heroOverview: Pasteurellosis is a bacterial infection caused primarily by Pasteurella multocida, a gram-negative coccobacillus found in the oral cavities of approximately 70–90% of cats and 50–70% of dogs worldwide. It is the most common cause of wound infection after animal bites in clinical practice, responsible for roughly 50% of infected dog-bite wounds and 75% of infected cat-bite wounds evaluated in emergency departments. Cats inflict deep puncture wounds that inoculate the organism directly into tissue, producing a characteristically rapid onset of local inflammation — often within 12–24 hours of the bite — that can progress to cellulitis, tenosynovitis, septic arthritis, or osteomyelitis. In immunocompromised individuals and people with chronic liver disease, Pasteurella can cause life-threatening bacteraemia and meningitis even without a preceding bite. Prompt wound care, antibiotic prophylaxis, and early treatment markedly reduce the risk of serious complications.

keyStats.prevalence: Responsible for approximately 75% of infected cat-bite wounds and 50% of infected dog-bite wounds in clinical series; approximately 4–5 million animal bites requiring medical care occur annually in the U.S. alone
keyStats.demographics: Affects all ages; children under 14 and the elderly are bitten most frequently; women are disproportionately affected by cat bites, while men account for most dog-bite injuries
keyStats.avgAge: All age groups at risk; children aged 5–14 have the highest incidence of dog bites; elderly patients have higher complication rates
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global pasteurellosis incidence is not tracked separately, but animal bites requiring care represent tens of millions of episodes annually worldwide

definition: Pasteurellosis (ICD-10: A28.0) is a bacterial infection caused by Pasteurella species, most commonly Pasteurella multocida subspecies multocida, and occasionally P. multocida subspecies septica, P. canis, P. stomatis, and P. dagmatis. These organisms are gram-negative, non-motile, bipolar-staining coccobacilli that colonise the upper respiratory tracts and oral cavities of domestic animals, particularly cats, dogs, pigs, rabbits, and cattle. In cats, the carriage rate approaches 70–90%; in dogs it is 50–70%. Human infection most commonly follows a bite or scratch that penetrates the skin deeply enough to inoculate the bacterium into subcutaneous tissue, tendon sheaths, or joints. The characteristic clinical presentation is a rapidly developing wound infection — erythema, swelling, and serous or purulent discharge — that begins within hours to 24 hours of the injury, an earlier onset than most other bite-wound pathogens such as Staphylococcus aureus or streptococci. Local spread can involve cellulitis, septic tenosynovitis (particularly in hand injuries from cat bites), septic arthritis, and osteomyelitis. Systemic dissemination causing bacteraemia, meningitis, peritonitis, endocarditis, or pneumonia occurs in immunosuppressed individuals, those with cirrhosis, or rarely in apparently healthy people. Pasteurellosis falls under infectious disease, though hand surgery, orthopaedics, and emergency medicine are frequently involved in wound management.

typesClassification.description: The most common presentation; rapid-onset erythema, swelling, tenderness, and serous or purulent exudate at the bite or scratch site, developing within 12–24 hours. Cat bites produce deep puncture wounds that are particularly prone to rapid progression.

typesClassification.type: Deep local infection (tenosynovitis, septic arthritis, osteomyelitis)
typesClassification.description: Cat and dog bites to the hand can inoculate Pasteurella into tendon sheaths and joints, causing septic tenosynovitis or arthritis; if untreated, osteomyelitis develops, particularly in finger bones adjacent to puncture wounds.

In immunocompromised individuals, those with liver disease, or occasionally immunocompetent people after heavy inoculum, Pasteurella enters the bloodstream, producing bacteraemia with fever, rigors, and the potential for seeding of distant organs including the meninges, lungs, and heart valves.

Rarely, Pasteurella causes pneumonia, lung abscess, or exacerbation of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease in people with close animal contact; this route does not require a bite and is associated with inhalation of animal respiratory secretions.

primarySymptoms: Rapid-onset pain, erythema, and swelling at the bite or scratch wound, appearing within 12–24 hours of injury — a distinctively faster onset than most other bite-wound infections, which typically evolve over 24–48 hours or longer.
Local warmth and induration at the wound site, with a firm swollen area surrounding the puncture mark or laceration.
Serous, serosanguinous, or purulent wound discharge, which may be minimal in deep cat puncture wounds despite significant subcutaneous infection.
Spreading redness tracking along skin (cutaneous lymphangitis) or spreading beyond the initial wound margins, indicating progressive cellulitis.
Regional lymph node enlargement and tenderness in the axilla, elbow crease, or groin depending on the bite location.
Fever and systemic features in more severe cases — temperature above 38°C with malaise and, in complicated infections, rigors.
Limited range of motion and disproportionate pain on passive extension of the fingers following a cat bite to the hand, suggesting tendon sheath involvement (septic tenosynovitis).
Joint swelling, warmth, and reduced range of motion when a bite is close to or directly involves a joint space, pointing toward septic arthritis.
Persistent or worsening bone pain at the bite site 1–2 weeks after the initial injury, suggesting osteomyelitis.
In systemic disease: high fever, chills, confusion, and clinical deterioration suggesting bacteraemia, particularly in immunocompromised or liver-disease patients.
earlyWarningSigns: Any wound from a cat or dog bite that becomes red, swollen, or painful within 12 hours of the injury — this rapid timeline is characteristic of Pasteurella.
A bite wound that appears small but produces deep, disproportionate pain, suggesting a puncture into a tendon sheath or joint.
Mild redness or warmth spreading beyond the wound edge in the first few hours after a cat bite.
Fever developing within 24 hours of a bite wound, indicating that bacteria have entered lymphatics or the bloodstream.
emergencySigns: Disproportionate pain on passive extension of any finger following a hand bite — a hallmark of septic tenosynovitis requiring emergency surgical assessment.
High fever with hypotension, confusion, or rapid pulse suggesting Pasteurella bacteraemia or septic shock.
A rapidly expanding area of redness or a dark, dusky discolouration of skin around the wound suggesting severe deep-tissue infection or necrotising fasciitis.
Any bite wound with fever in an immunocompromised patient, someone with cirrhosis, or a patient on immunosuppressive medication — these patients can decompensate rapidly.
Seizure, severe headache, or neck stiffness following a bite and fever, suggesting meningitis.

causes.description: Cats inflict narrow, deep puncture wounds that penetrate into tendon sheaths and periosteum, depositing Pasteurella multocida — which colonises the oral cavity of 70–90% of healthy cats — deep in poorly vascularised tissue where it proliferates rapidly and is difficult to drain. Cat-bite infections are faster to progress and more likely to require hospitalisation than dog-bite infections.

Dogs carry P. multocida in 50–70% of cases; their bites tend to be crushing lacerations rather than deep punctures. The wider wound allows easier drainage but still inoculates the organism into tissue. Dog bites can also co-infect with Capnocytophaga canimorsus and other oral flora.

Even without a deep bite, a cat or dog scratch — particularly from a cat that has recently licked its claws — can introduce Pasteurella through the skin. Facial scratches in young children are a common mechanism.

Rabbits, pigs, cattle, horses, and rarely birds carry Pasteurella species and can transmit them through bites, scratches, or close respiratory contact; farm and veterinary workers are at risk through these routes.

In individuals with underlying lung disease such as chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, Pasteurella can cause respiratory infection through inhalation of animal respiratory secretions without a bite, producing pneumonia or exacerbating existing lung disease.

riskFactors.factor: Cat or dog ownership and exposure
riskFactors.description: Owning or regularly handling cats — the most efficient vectors — or dogs is the dominant risk factor for pasteurellosis; even domestic pet bites from an animal that has received veterinary care carry significant Pasteurella carriage and infection risk.

Veterinarians, veterinary nurses, animal shelter workers, farm workers, and pet shop staff experience repeated animal bites and scratches; bite-reporting and prophylaxis protocols are essential in these settings.

HIV infection, organ transplantation, haematological malignancy, corticosteroid therapy, and chemotherapy substantially increase the risk of bacteraemia and systemic spread from a bite wound; even an uncomplicated-seeming bite can cause life-threatening sepsis in these patients.

Cirrhotic patients have impaired clearance of gram-negative bacteria and are at particularly high risk for fatal Pasteurella bacteraemia; any animal bite in a patient with liver disease warrants immediate antibiotic therapy and close observation.

Impaired neutrophil function and peripheral vascular disease in diabetes slow wound healing and increase the risk of deep-tissue infection, cellulitis extension, and osteomyelitis from animal bites; tight glucose control before and after an injury reduces risk.

Older adults have reduced immune responses, are more commonly on immunosuppressive medications, and have a higher prevalence of underlying conditions (liver disease, diabetes) that amplify Pasteurella infection risk.

Hand bites — particularly to the fingers and dorsum of the hand — carry the highest risk of Pasteurella tenosynovitis, septic arthritis, and osteomyelitis because tendon sheaths and joints lie immediately beneath the skin; all hand bites deserve urgent assessment.

affectedDemographics: Children aged 5–14, the most frequently bitten group, especially by dogs
Women, who are bitten by cats disproportionately and sustain a higher proportion of infected cat-bite injuries
Elderly individuals with comorbidities including liver disease and immunosuppression
Veterinarians, veterinary nurses, and animal shelter and farm workers
Immunocompromised patients including those with HIV, transplant recipients, and haematological malignancy
People with chronic liver disease or cirrhosis, in whom bacteraemia risk is greatly elevated
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of pasteurellosis begins with the injury history: any patient with an animal bite or scratch developing local infection within 24 hours should be assessed for Pasteurella infection. The clinical presentation — rapid-onset wound inflammation after a cat or dog bite — is sufficiently distinctive to start empirical antibiotics without awaiting culture in most cases. Wound culture from a deep swab, wound fluid, or aspirated material identifies the organism and provides antibiotic sensitivities in 24–48 hours; the lab should be informed of the bite history because Pasteurella requires identification beyond standard gram-negative identification panels. Blood cultures are essential when systemic signs (fever above 38.5°C, rigors, confusion, or haemodynamic instability) are present. In suspected tendon sheath or joint involvement, urgent surgical assessment and joint aspiration are needed; imaging with X-ray is done to look for bony erosion and with ultrasound or MRI to assess tendon sheath involvement. MRI is the most sensitive modality for detecting osteomyelitis. In respiratory pasteurellosis, sputum culture or bronchoscopic sampling is used. Laboratory tests show leucocytosis, elevated CRP, and raised inflammatory markers in proportion to disease severity. P. multocida is a small gram-negative rod (or coccobacillus) on Gram stain, and can be definitively identified by MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry in modern laboratories. The key differentials include other bite-wound bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus species, anaerobes), Capnocytophaga canimorsus (especially in asplenic patients), cat-scratch disease from Bartonella henselae, and necrotising fasciitis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies Pasteurella and other bite-wound organisms, provides antibiotic susceptibility data to guide therapy or modify empirical treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A sterile swab is inserted into the depth of the wound, or fluid is aspirated by a physician; the sample is sent to the microbiology laboratory. Culture results are typically available in 24–48 hours. Informing the laboratory of the animal bite history helps ensure appropriate identification.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects bacteraemia in patients with systemic fever, rigors, or haemodynamic instability after a bite wound, and guides intravenous antibiotic selection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two to three sets of blood drawn from different venepuncture sites; standard automated culture systems can detect Pasteurella within 24–72 hours. Inform the laboratory of the clinical context.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies bone erosion or periosteal reaction suggesting osteomyelitis, and may detect an unsuspected foreign body such as a tooth fragment in a deep laceration.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard plain X-ray of the affected hand, finger, or limb; takes a few minutes and involves low radiation. Early osteomyelitis may not be visible on X-ray until 10–14 days after infection onset.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The most sensitive imaging modality for detecting tenosynovitis, deep abscess, and osteomyelitis; indicated when clinical signs suggest tendon or bone involvement.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30–60 minute scan in an MRI machine; no radiation. Contrast dye may be injected intravenously to enhance visualisation of inflamed tissue.

diagnosticTests.test: Joint aspiration and synovial fluid analysis
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms septic arthritis, identifies the causative organism, and relieves pressure in the joint space when significant effusion is present.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A physician inserts a sterile needle into the joint under local anaesthesia; the aspirated fluid is sent for culture, cell count, and Gram stain. Results take 24–48 hours for culture; the white cell count result is available within hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents leucocytosis and systemic inflammation proportional to infection severity, and provides a baseline to monitor treatment response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results available within 2–4 hours at most hospitals.

treatmentOverview: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is the first-line oral antibiotic for pasteurellosis and animal-bite wound infections because it covers Pasteurella multocida, as well as the range of oral bacteria (Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, anaerobes) present in bite wounds. For uncomplicated local wound infection, amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily or 500/125 mg three times daily for 5–7 days is standard. Prophylactic antibiotics are recommended for all cat bites, high-risk dog bites (hands, face, deep wounds, immunocompromised hosts), and any bite in a patient with asplenia, liver disease, or significant immunosuppression — ideally started within 12 hours of injury. Penicillin V alone (or IV penicillin G for hospitalised patients) is highly active against Pasteurella specifically but has poor coverage of staphylococci and is generally insufficient as monotherapy for mixed bite-wound infections. For penicillin-allergic patients, doxycycline covers Pasteurella and many bite-wound organisms, but moxifloxacin or a combination regimen should be considered after allergy specialist advice. Fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin) have variable activity against anaerobes and should not be used as bite-wound monotherapy. Septic tenosynovitis and septic arthritis require emergency surgical irrigation and debridement in addition to intravenous antibiotics (typically amoxicillin-clavulanate or piperacillin-tazobactam), as antibiotics alone are insufficient for these deep compartment infections. Bacteraemia and endocarditis are treated with intravenous penicillin G or amoxicillin for 4–6 weeks following endocarditis guidelines. All bite wounds should receive thorough irrigation and debridement; most cat bites are left open or loosely approximated rather than primarily closed because of the high infection risk.

medicalTreatments.name: Amoxicillin-clavulanate (oral)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line broad-spectrum antibiotic for bite-wound infections; covers Pasteurella multocida, staphylococci, streptococci, and oral anaerobes in a single agent. Given as 875/125 mg twice daily or 500/125 mg three times daily for 5–7 days for prophylaxis or uncomplicated infection; 10–14 days for deeper infections.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure for uncomplicated bite-wound cellulitis in approximately 85–95% of cases; widely recommended by IDSA bite-wound guidelines as the preferred single agent.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous amoxicillin-clavulanate or piperacillin-tazobactam
medicalTreatments.description: For hospitalised patients with spreading cellulitis, suspected tenosynovitis, bacteraemia, or systemic signs; provides broad coverage while awaiting culture results. Piperacillin-tazobactam is preferred for critically ill patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical improvement within 48–72 hours expected in 80–90% of hospitalised bite-wound infections with appropriate IV therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Penicillin G (intravenous)
medicalTreatments.description: Highly active against Pasteurella multocida and used for targeted therapy once cultures confirm Pasteurella as the sole pathogen, or for bacteraemia and endocarditis; typically 1–2 million units every 4–6 hours. Not sufficient as monotherapy for mixed bite-wound infections.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Bacteriological cure rate greater than 95% for susceptible Pasteurella bacteraemia with a complete intravenous course.

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline (oral)
medicalTreatments.description: The preferred alternative for penicillin-allergic adult patients; 100 mg twice daily for 7–14 days depending on infection severity. Has activity against Pasteurella and most bite-wound aerobes but limited anaerobic coverage.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure rates of approximately 80–90% for uncomplicated bite-wound infections in penicillin-allergic patients completing the full course.

medicalTreatments.name: Wound irrigation, debridement, and surgical drainage
medicalTreatments.description: Not an antibiotic but a critical component of treatment for all bite wounds; thorough irrigation with normal saline under pressure removes bacterial inoculum, and debridement of devitalised tissue reduces the bacterial burden. Septic tenosynovitis and septic arthritis require urgent surgical irrigation and drainage.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Irrigation and debridement combined with antibiotics reduce bite-wound infection rates by approximately 50–70% compared to antibiotics alone in controlled studies.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical irrigation and debridement of bite wound
surgicalOptions.description: Formal wound exploration, irrigation, and removal of devitalised tissue under sterile conditions; required for all deep cat or dog bites to the hand and face that cannot be adequately managed at the bedside.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Infection resolution achieved in approximately 85–90% of cases when combined with appropriate IV antibiotics for deep hand infections.

surgicalOptions.name: Tendon sheath irrigation (septic tenosynovitis)
surgicalOptions.description: Emergency surgical procedure in which a catheter is inserted into the tendon sheath and irrigated with saline under pressure; essential for septic flexor tenosynovitis, where delays of more than 24 hours significantly worsen hand function outcomes.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Full hand function preserved in approximately 75–85% of cases when performed within 24–48 hours of symptom onset; delayed surgery results in much higher rates of functional impairment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Topical antiseptic irrigation at point of care
alternativeTreatments.description: Irrigation of the fresh bite wound with dilute povidone-iodine, chlorhexidine, or copious normal saline reduces the surface bacterial load before formal medical assessment; not a substitute for systemic antibiotics in high-risk bites but reduces bacterial inoculum at the entry point.

alternativeTreatments.name: Tetanus toxoid and immunoglobulin
alternativeTreatments.description: Any animal bite warrants review of tetanus immunisation status and administration of tetanus toxoid or immunoglobulin according to current vaccination status and wound characteristics. This is standard bite management, not an alternative to antibiotic therapy.

whySeeSpecialist: Any bite wound to the hand from a cat or dog should be urgently assessed by a physician — ideally an emergency physician or hand surgeon — because tendon sheath involvement can develop within hours and causes lasting functional impairment if not drained promptly. Infectious disease consultation is appropriate when bacteraemia is suspected, when the patient is immunocompromised, when wounds fail to improve within 48 hours of antibiotics, or when endocarditis is a concern. Orthopaedic or plastic surgery input is needed for osteomyelitis, septic arthritis, and deep-space hand infections.
doctorSelectionGuide: For acute bite wounds, the emergency department is the appropriate first point of contact; a hand surgeon should be involved immediately for any bite to the hand with reduced range of motion or suspicion of tendon involvement. For ongoing or complicated infections, an infectious disease specialist guides antibiotic duration and adjustment. Immunocompromised patients benefit from combined management between infectious disease and their primary specialist (oncologist, transplant physician, or HIV physician).
preventionStrategies: Immediately wash any cat or dog bite with soap and water for at least 5 minutes, then irrigate with antiseptic and seek medical assessment within a few hours for cat bites and high-risk dog bites.
All cat bites and high-risk dog bites should receive prophylactic amoxicillin-clavulanate for 5 days, ideally started within 12 hours of the injury.
Immunocompromised individuals, those with liver disease, and asplenic patients should receive antibiotic prophylaxis after any animal bite, however minor, and seek same-day care.
Teach children not to disturb animals that are eating, sleeping, or protecting young; supervise all interactions between children and pets.
Veterinary workers should use bite-proof gloves when handling stressed or restrained animals and report all bites per workplace protocol.
Ensure tetanus vaccination is current before anticipated high-risk animal contact.
lifestyleModifications: Report any animal bite to a healthcare provider within 24 hours; cat bites in particular should not be self-managed and observed due to the high rate of rapid infection.
Elevate and rest the bitten limb, particularly the hand, to reduce oedema and limit spread of infection.
Complete the full prescribed antibiotic course even when the wound looks improved; stopping early risks relapse or development of deep infection.
Monitor the wound daily for changes in redness, swelling, or discharge, and return immediately if these worsen or fever develops.
Keep pet vaccination up to date and maintain regular veterinary care for all household animals.

recommended: Adequate protein intake to support wound healing and immune response during recovery
Vitamin C-rich foods, which support collagen synthesis and wound repair
Adequate hydration to support antibiotic distribution and recovery
avoid: Alcohol, which impairs immune function, wound healing, and may interact with doxycycline or metronidazole
Dairy products within two hours of doxycycline doses if doxycycline is prescribed
Excessive sugar, particularly relevant in patients with diabetes, where blood glucose control is critical for wound healing

exerciseGuidelines: The bitten limb, especially the hand, should be rested and elevated during the acute phase of infection. Avoid any activity that flexes or stresses the infected area. Once infection has resolved and surgical clearance given for hand injuries, graduated physiotherapy or occupational therapy may be recommended to restore range of motion, particularly after tenosynovitis. Full return to normal activity should follow clinical and, where applicable, surgical clearance.
dailyManagement: Complete the entire prescribed antibiotic course without interruption, regardless of symptomatic improvement.
Inspect the wound at least daily and document any changes in erythema, swelling, discharge, or pain.
Return immediately to the clinic or emergency department if you develop fever above 38°C, increased pain, worsening redness, or limited finger movement.
Keep the wound covered with a clean dressing and change it at least once daily.
For hand bites, immobilise and elevate the hand as directed and follow up with the treating physician within 24–48 hours of initial assessment.
prognosis: The prognosis for uncomplicated local pasteurellosis treated promptly with amoxicillin-clavulanate is excellent, with resolution expected in 5–14 days. Cat-bite hand infections treated with intravenous antibiotics and, when needed, surgical drainage also carry a generally favourable outcome, though 10–30% of patients with septic flexor tenosynovitis experience some residual stiffness or reduced grip strength. Bacteraemia in immunocompetent individuals treated promptly resolves fully in over 90% of cases. Mortality is low in healthy adults but rises substantially in immunocompromised or cirrhotic patients with bacteraemia, where case-fatality rates of 20–30% have been reported in case series. Endocarditis requires prolonged treatment but is curable in most cases diagnosed before irreversible valve destruction occurs. Osteomyelitis may require 4–6 weeks of antibiotics and occasionally surgery, with good long-term outcomes when treated before bone necrosis sets in.
recoveryTimeline: Uncomplicated local wound infections typically show improvement within 24–48 hours of starting amoxicillin-clavulanate, with most cases fully resolved within 7–14 days. Deep-tissue infections including tenosynovitis respond more slowly: fever usually settles in 48–72 hours of IV antibiotics and surgery, but hand function may require weeks to months of physiotherapy to recover. Bacteraemia generally resolves within 48–72 hours of effective IV antibiotics. Osteomyelitis requires 4–6 weeks of antibiotics, with pain improvement in the first 1–2 weeks. Endocarditis follows a 4–6 week intravenous course with convalescence extending several months.
complications: Septic flexor tenosynovitis of the hand — a surgical emergency causing severe pain on passive extension and requiring urgent tendon sheath irrigation; delayed treatment results in permanent finger stiffness in up to 30% of cases.
Septic arthritis — bacterial joint infection causing pain, swelling, and cartilage destruction if not drained and treated promptly with intravenous antibiotics.
Osteomyelitis — infection of bone adjacent to the bite wound, particularly phalanges; requires 4–6 weeks of antibiotics and sometimes surgical debridement.
Bacteraemia and septic shock — life-threatening in immunocompromised or cirrhotic patients; reported mortality of 20–30% in highest-risk groups.
Meningitis — rare but severe complication, particularly after facial bites or in immunosuppressed patients, presenting with severe headache, neck stiffness, and altered consciousness.
Infective endocarditis — Pasteurella seeding heart valves during bacteraemia; requires 4–6 weeks of intravenous penicillin and carries significant morbidity.

supportResources.name: CDC — Animal Bites Prevention
supportResources.description: CDC guidance on preventing animal-bite injuries and infections in occupational and domestic settings.

supportResources.name: NHS — Animal and Human Bites
supportResources.description: NHS patient guidance on assessing bite severity, wound care, antibiotic prophylaxis, and when to seek emergency care after an animal bite.

supportResources.name: IDSA — Management of Skin and Soft Tissue Infections Guidelines
supportResources.description: Infectious Diseases Society of America clinical practice guidelines covering animal bite wound infections and antibiotic recommendations.

sources.title: Talan DA et al. Bacteriologic Analysis of Infected Dog and Cat Bites. New England Journal of Medicine, 1999
Abrahamian FM, Goldstein EJ. Microbiology of animal bite wound infections. Clinical Microbiology Reviews, 2011
Stevens DL et al. IDSA Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Skin and Soft Tissue Infections, 2014 update
Goldstein EJ. Bite wounds and infection. Clinical Infectious Diseases, 1992
NHS — Animal and Human Bites Clinical Guidance
CDC — National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health: Animals in the Workplace
Medeiros I, Saconato H. Antibiotic prophylaxis for mammalian bites. Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews, 2001

confusedWithConditions.name: Cat-scratch disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cat-scratch disease is caused by Bartonella henselae and presents with a slowly developing papule at the scratch site followed by prominent regional lymph node swelling 1–3 weeks later; it does not cause the rapid-onset wound cellulitis or deep-tissue infection characteristic of Pasteurella.

confusedWithConditions.name: Leptospirosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Leptospirosis is acquired from water or soil contaminated with rodent urine, not animal bites; it causes severe myalgia, jaundice, and kidney injury, not rapid bite-wound cellulitis, and is diagnosed by serology.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tularemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tularemia can follow animal bites or ticks and causes an ulcer at the inoculation site with painful regional lymph nodes (ulceroglandular form), but the onset is slower than Pasteurella cellulitis and diagnosis requires specific serology or culture on special media.

confusedWithConditions.name: Erysipelothrix sepsis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Erysipelothrix rhusiopathiae causes erysipeloid — a slowly spreading violaceous skin lesion after handling fish or pork — rather than the rapid bite-wound cellulitis of Pasteurella; the exposures and clinical progression are distinct.

relatedConditions.name: Cat-scratch disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Another common infection from cat contact, caused by Bartonella henselae; distinguishing the two is clinically important because cat-scratch disease presents weeks later with lymphadenopathy rather than rapid bite-wound cellulitis.

relatedConditions.name: Tularemia
relatedConditions.relevance: A bacterial zoonosis that can follow animal bites or tick bites and is an important differential when bite-wound infection presents atypically with ulceration and lymphadenopathy.

relatedConditions.name: Leptospirosis
relatedConditions.relevance: A rodent-associated zoonosis considered in animal workers with systemic febrile illness after any animal contact; distinguished from pasteurellosis by the absence of rapid wound cellulitis and the presence of jaundice and kidney injury.

relatedConditions.name: Anthrax
relatedConditions.relevance: A zoonosis that can present as cutaneous infection after animal contact, an important differential in occupational exposures to livestock with fever and skin lesion.

faqs.question: What is pasteurellosis?
faqs.answer: Pasteurellosis is a bacterial infection caused by Pasteurella multocida, found in the mouths of 70–90% of cats and 50–70% of dogs. It is the most common cause of infected animal bite wounds, producing rapid-onset wound redness, swelling, and pain within 12–24 hours of a bite.

faqs.question: How quickly does a cat bite get infected with Pasteurella?
faqs.answer: Pasteurella infections develop remarkably fast — often within 12–24 hours of a cat bite. This rapid onset distinguishes it from other bite infections like Staphylococcus, which typically take 24–72 hours to produce visible signs. Any redness or pain after a cat bite within a day warrants medical assessment.

faqs.question: What antibiotic treats pasteurellosis?
faqs.answer: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is the standard first-line antibiotic for both prophylaxis and treatment of animal bite infections because it covers Pasteurella, staphylococci, streptococci, and oral anaerobes in a single agent. For penicillin-allergic patients, doxycycline is the usual alternative.

faqs.question: Is a cat bite dangerous?
faqs.answer: Cat bites are considered higher-risk than dog bites because cats inflict deep, narrow puncture wounds that inoculate bacteria directly into tendon sheaths and joints. Approximately 30–50% of cat bites that are not treated with prophylactic antibiotics become infected. Bites to the hand are especially serious.

faqs.question: Do I need antibiotics after a cat bite?
faqs.answer: Yes, for virtually all cat bites. Clinical guidelines recommend prophylactic amoxicillin-clavulanate for 5 days for all cat bites, ideally starting within 12 hours of injury. Cat bites have a very high infection rate, and the infections can progress rapidly to deep tissue, tendon, or joint involvement.

faqs.question: What should I do immediately after a cat or dog bite?
faqs.answer: Wash the wound thoroughly with soap and water for at least 5 minutes, apply antiseptic, and see a doctor promptly — within a few hours for cat bites to the hand, within 24 hours for other bites. Antibiotic prophylaxis, wound assessment, and a tetanus check will be performed.

faqs.question: What is septic tenosynovitis from a cat bite?
faqs.answer: Septic tenosynovitis is an infection inside the tendon sheath — the fluid-filled tube surrounding the tendons in the finger. A cat bite can inoculate bacteria directly into this space, causing severe pain on passive extension of the finger. It is a surgical emergency requiring immediate tendon sheath washout.

faqs.question: Can Pasteurella cause serious illness?
faqs.answer: In healthy individuals, Pasteurella usually causes local wound infection. In immunocompromised patients, those with liver disease, or people with asplenia, it can cause life-threatening bacteraemia, meningitis, or endocarditis. These patients need immediate antibiotics after any animal bite.

faqs.question: Can you get pasteurellosis without being bitten?
faqs.answer: Yes, in rare cases. Close respiratory contact with animals carrying Pasteurella in their airways can cause pneumonia, particularly in people with pre-existing lung disease. Facial licking by pets is also a possible, though uncommon, route of transmission to the mouth or eyes.

faqs.question: How is pasteurellosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is usually clinical — rapid-onset wound inflammation after a cat or dog bite is the key finding. Wound culture or blood culture confirms the organism. The laboratory must be told about the bite history because Pasteurella requires specific identification protocols.

faqs.question: Is pasteurellosis the same as cat-scratch disease?
faqs.answer: No. Cat-scratch disease is caused by Bartonella henselae and presents weeks after a scratch with prominent swollen lymph nodes, not rapid wound cellulitis. Pasteurellosis causes rapid local infection at the bite or scratch site itself. Both come from cat contact but are different bacteria with different clinical pictures.

faqs.question: Can dog bites cause pasteurellosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dogs carry Pasteurella multocida in approximately 50–70% of cases. Dog-bite infections are less likely to progress as rapidly as cat bites because dogs produce crushing lacerations rather than narrow punctures, but Pasteurella is still a major pathogen in infected dog-bite wounds.

faqs.question: What makes cat bites more dangerous than dog bites?
faqs.answer: Cats have narrow, sharp teeth that create deep puncture wounds rather than surface lacerations. These punctures are hard to clean, seal quickly over bacteria, and reach directly into tendon sheaths and joints. Dog bites are wider lacerations that allow easier drainage, though both require prompt medical assessment.

faqs.question: How long does pasteurellosis treatment take?
faqs.answer: Uncomplicated local infections are treated for 5–7 days with oral amoxicillin-clavulanate. Deeper infections involving tendons or bone may require 10–14 days of antibiotics, with intravenous therapy initially. Bacteraemia is treated intravenously for 10–14 days, and endocarditis for 4–6 weeks.

faqs.question: Can pasteurellosis be prevented?
faqs.answer: All cat bites and high-risk dog bites should receive prophylactic amoxicillin-clavulanate within 12 hours. Immediate thorough wound washing reduces bacterial inoculum. Immunocompromised individuals and those with liver disease should treat any animal bite as an emergency.

faqs.question: Should I go to the emergency room for an animal bite?
faqs.answer: Go immediately for any bite to the hand that limits finger movement or causes severe deep pain, any bite with signs of spreading redness, any bite in an immunocompromised person, and any bite with fever. Cat bites to the hand are particularly urgent due to the high risk of tendon sheath infection.

faqs.question: Can children get pasteurellosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children under 14 are the most frequently bitten group, primarily by dogs, and are at risk for pasteurellosis from both dogs and cats. All animal bites in children should receive medical assessment within 24 hours, with antibiotic prophylaxis for high-risk bites.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for pasteurellosis?
faqs.answer: With prompt antibiotics, uncomplicated pasteurellosis resolves fully in 7–14 days. Deep hand infections treated surgically have good outcomes but may cause some residual stiffness. Bacteraemia in healthy adults has a very good prognosis; in immunocompromised or cirrhotic patients, mortality can reach 20–30%.

faqs.question: Does Pasteurella show up in routine blood tests?
faqs.answer: Routine blood tests (CBC, CRP) show leucocytosis and elevated inflammatory markers but do not identify the organism. Diagnosis requires wound culture or blood culture with laboratory notification of the bite history. PCR-based identification by MALDI-TOF in modern labs can give results faster than traditional culture.

faqs.question: Why is hand hygiene important for pet owners?
faqs.answer: Washing hands with soap and water after handling pets, particularly after cleaning litter boxes, changing bedding, or following any scratch or bite, removes potentially infective secretions from the skin before they can enter minor wounds or reach the face. It is the simplest preventive measure against pasteurellosis and other zoonoses.

metaDescription: Pasteurellosis from cat and dog bites causes rapid wound infection within 24 hours. Learn symptoms, antibiotic treatment and when to seek emergency care.
